text: After introducing the ports, the surgeon uses cold scissors for sharp dissection to navigate and remove dense adhesions obscuring the gallbladder. The suction device is employed to assist in dissection with minimal trauma, allowing for clearer visualization of the gallbladder. The surgeon then initiates posterior dissection.

The surgeon identifies an unusually long and wide cystic duct, initially mistaken for the common bile duct (CBD). The cystic duct is looped twice, with visible bile present, indicating an anomalous biliary-pancreatic junction.

The surgeon gently teases the gallbladder away from the liver bed, carefully separating the two structures. A drain is placed to manage potential bile leakage, which is crucial in cases with anomalous anatomy.

The surgeon encounters extensive adhesions forming a pseudomembrane across the liver, obscuring the view of the liver itself. The XLC duct of Dushka is identified and clipped, marking the completion of the procedure.
